questions.question: A weld was made by GTAW and shows a small, tight, dense internal reflector. Which discontinuity is essentially unique to this process?
options: Slag inclusion from the electrode coating
Tungsten inclusion from the electrode dipping into the puddle
questions.explanation: GTAW uses a non-consumable tungsten electrode; touching it into the puddle leaves a dense tungsten fragment. GTAW forms no slag because it uses no flux.

questions.question: With a 45° probe on 30 mm plate (cos 45° ≈ 0.707), what is the sound-path length of one leg?
questions.explanation: Leg = T/cosθ = 30/0.707 ≈ 42.4 mm of sound path from the index point down to the far surface.

questions.question: Why is the root of a thick double-V weld commonly back-gouged before the second side is welded?
options: To increase the included angle for easier scanning
To remove the first side's root region — taking incomplete penetration and root defects out before they are buried at mid-thickness
To eliminate the need for UT after welding
questions.explanation: Gouging to sound metal removes the unfused or defective first root; otherwise those discontinuities end up trapped near mid-thickness where the two sides meet.

questions.question: Where do crater cracks form, and why?
options: Along the weld toes, from undercut stress concentration
In the base metal under the weld, from through-thickness strain
At mid-thickness of double-V welds, from missed back-gouging
At abrupt arc stops, where the last small puddle solidifies under shrinkage and tears in a star pattern
questions.explanation: Ending the arc abruptly leaves a small unfilled crater whose last liquid tears as it shrinks — the same hot-cracking mechanism as centerline cracking, concentrated at the stop.

questions.question: Which of these is a surface/geometry condition rather than an embedded discontinuity?
questions.explanation: Undercut is a groove melted into the base-metal surface at the toe — visible from outside and a source of honest geometric echoes, unlike embedded LOF, slag, or porosity.

questions.question: Hydrogen (cold) cracking requires which combination?
options: High sulfur, fast travel speed, and a deep narrow bead
Tungsten contamination and excessive shielding gas
Hydrogen, a hardened susceptible microstructure (typically HAZ), and tensile stress
questions.explanation: All three ingredients must coexist — hydrogen from moisture or contamination, a crack-susceptible hardened zone, and shrinkage/restraint stress. Remove any one and cold cracking is prevented.

questions.question: An embedded indication gives a broad, somewhat ragged, medium-amplitude echo of similar height from both sides of the weld and is not very sensitive to small angle changes. The most likely reflector is:
questions.explanation: Slag's irregular surface reflects diffusely: medium, broad, ragged, and roughly symmetric from both sides. Smooth LOF is strongly one-sided and angle-sensitive; a bead plots at the root surface, not embedded.

questions.question: A 70° probe (cos 70° ≈ 0.342, sin 70° ≈ 0.940) shows a first-leg reflector at 40 mm sound path in 20 mm plate. The reflector depth is about:
questions.explanation: Depth in leg 1 = s·cosθ = 40 × 0.342 ≈ 13.7 mm (valid since it is less than T). The 37.6 mm figure is the surface distance, s·sinθ.

questions.question: You detect an indication while scanning at reference + 6 dB. Before comparing its amplitude to the DAC curve you must:
options: Add another 6 dB to be conservative
Switch to a higher frequency probe
Return the gain to the primary reference level used to build the DAC
questions.explanation: Scanning gain exists only to avoid missing signals; evaluation is valid solely at the calibrated reference sensitivity the DAC was recorded at.

questions.question: A project invokes fitness-for-service (fracture-mechanics) acceptance instead of workmanship criteria. Which capability becomes essential?
options: Accurate through-wall height sizing of planar flaws, typically using tip-diffraction-based techniques
questions.explanation: FFS analysis turns on actual flaw dimensions — especially height — and character, so quantitative sizing (TOFD/PAUT tip methods) replaces simple amplitude-versus-reference logic.
